skill.name: subagent-factory
skill.description: Create specialized Claude Code agent definitions with guided workflows, scoped tools, frontmatter, and prompt patterns.
skill.summary: Build focused Claude Code subagents with clear responsibilities, tool access, and reusable prompts.
skill.author: AaronAbuUsama

security_audit.summary: The static scan reported many command, filesystem, network, and secret patterns, but review shows they are mainly documentation examples for Claude Code agents, hooks, slash commands, and MCP setup. No evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection was found. The main publish concern is that some examples show powerful agent permissions, persistent agent locations, credentials, and one unsafe command-execution sample that users could copy.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Command Execution Example
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The MCP example imports child_process and runs execSync with a command string built from request arguments. This is dangerous if copied into a real MCP server because unvalidated input can alter shell execution. The risk is reduced because it is documentation, not active skill code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is explicit and includes request-controlled data. Confidence is not higher because the code appears in a reference example rather than running as part of the skill.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Agent Tool Grants and Persistent Agent Storage
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill teaches creation of agents that can receive Bash, Write, Edit, WebFetch, and user-level storage in ~/.claude/agents. These are legitimate capabilities, but broad grants can create persistent high-privilege agent behavior if users select them casually.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is clearly documented and relevant to the skill purpose. The risk depends on user choices, so it is a configuration hazard rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Environment Variable Examples
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: Several MCP and hook examples reference API keys, database URLs, GitHub tokens, and Google credentials. They are placeholders or normal SDK usage, but users should avoid committing these values or exposing them through shell history and logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly reference sensitive environment variables, but the values shown are placeholders or standard credential-loading examples. Human review should ensure marketplace warnings tell users not to paste real secrets into agent files.

low_findings.title: Security Terminology Triggered Sensitive Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static findings for weak cryptography, C2 keywords, Windows SAM, and attack wording mostly come from security-auditor prompts and checklists. The text is defensive guidance for reviewers, not credential theft or malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are checklist or expertise descriptions for security review agents. They do not instruct exfiltration, exploitation, or bypass activity.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Access Is Agent Configuration Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hidden path findings point to documented Claude Code locations such as .claude/agents and ~/.claude/agents. The skill explains where agent definitions live and does not access those paths by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are path documentation and user workflow instructions. No hidden file read, write, or exfiltration behavior is present in the skill content itself.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Network URL findings are mostly links to Claude, Anthropic, GitHub, and community documentation. They are resource references, not runtime callbacks or data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in resource sections and are not embedded in executable skill logic. One curl example is separately covered as credential-related documentation risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Built From Request Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The MCP example constructs a shell command using request.params.arguments before execSync. Users should replace this with argument arrays, validation, or a safe API wrapper.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolated request value is visible next to execSync. The risk is contextual because it is sample code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Tool Sets for Generated Agents
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples include Bash, Write, Edit, WebFetch, and MCP tools. These are useful but should be minimized for each agent role.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly include powerful tools. The skill also advises explicit tool scoping, which lowers the overall concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Placeholders in Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show API key and database URL variables in MCP and hook commands. Users should store secrets in secure environment management, not committed agent files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive variable names are explicit, but no real secret values are present in the reviewed lines.

content.value_statement: Custom agent files are easy to make incorrectly, which can cause vague behavior or excessive tool access. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users through focused agent definitions, prompts, and tool scoping.
actual_capabilities: Guides quick creation of Claude Code agent definition files.
Provides an interview workflow for complex agent design.
Explains required frontmatter fields and Markdown prompt structure.
Suggests tool scopes for reviewers, researchers, implementers, and full-stack agents.
Includes prompt patterns, checklists, and examples for specialized agents.
Documents agent storage locations and Task tool invocation patterns.
limitations: It does not create agents automatically without user direction.
It is focused on Claude Code agent files, not general chatbot deployment.
Some reference examples require careful security review before reuse.
Generated agents still need testing against real project tasks.

use_cases.title: Create a Code Review Agent
use_cases.description: Define a read-only reviewer with clear triggers, security checks, and limited tools.

use_cases.title: Design a Research Agent
use_cases.description: Build an agent that searches documentation, summarizes findings, and stores notes only when needed.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Agent Patterns
use_cases.description: Create shared project agents with consistent names, descriptions, prompts, and tool boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Quick Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a simple agent with narrow scope.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code agent named test-runner. It should run tests, diagnose failures, and suggest fixes. Use minimal tools.

prompt_templates.title: Guided Interview
prompt_templates.scenario: Explore requirements before writing the agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Interview me to design a security review agent. Ask about purpose, triggers, tools, model, outputs, and boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Refine Existing Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve an agent definition that already exists.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current agent definition for clarity, tool scope, trigger quality, and missing workflow steps.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Agent Suite
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan several agents with different responsibilities.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a small agent suite for a web application team. Include reviewer, tester, researcher, and implementer agents with separate tool scopes.

output_examples.input: Create a read-only security reviewer agent for authentication changes.
output_examples.output: A proposed agent name, trigger description, read-only tool list, model choice, workflow checklist, and security-focused prompt outline.

output_examples.input: Help me build an agent for test failures in a TypeScript repository.
output_examples.output: A focused test-runner design with responsibilities, safe Bash usage guidance, failure triage steps, and expected report format.

output_examples.input: Interview me for a research agent that can use web tools.
output_examples.output: A sequence of design questions covering research scope, allowed sources, output format, storage needs, and escalation rules.

best_practices: Give each agent one clear responsibility and a precise trigger description.
Grant only the tools required for the agent role.
Test new agents on realistic tasks before sharing them with a team.
anti_patterns: Do not give every agent Bash, Write, and WebFetch by default.
Do not hide important instructions in vague prose or frontmatter comments.
Do not paste real secrets into agent files, examples, or prompts.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code agent definition files with frontmatter and Markdown system prompts.

faq.question: Can Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Codex can use the guidance to draft or review Claude Code agent files in a repository.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation and workflow guidance. Generated agents may receive command tools if the user grants them.

faq.question: Where are agent files stored?
faq.answer: Project agents usually live in .claude/agents. Personal agents can live in ~/.claude/agents.

faq.question: How should tools be selected?
faq.answer: Start with read-only tools. Add Write, Bash, WebFetch, or MCP tools only when the agent role requires them.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: Users can create agents with broad permissions. Review tool access and prompts before using agents on sensitive projects.
